closest_first_principle_concept_applied:

Let
  P(x) = x^5 + x^3 - x^2 - 1 \in ℤ[x].
Compute the discriminant Disc(P) modulo 1000, i.e. find the unique integer in {0,1,...,999} congruent to Disc(P) (mod 1000).

Solution.
Step 1 (factor_by_rational_root)
Check for an integer root using the Factor Theorem:
  P(1) = 1 + 1 - 1 - 1 = 0,
so (x-1) divides P(x). Write
  P(x) = (x-1)Q(x)
with Q monic of degree 4.

Perform the division (or expand backwards) to get
  Q(x) = x^4 + x^3 + 2x^2 + x + 1.

Step 2 (factor_quartic_into_quadratics)
Factor Q(x) further over ℤ:
  (x^2+1)(x^2+x+1)
= x^4 + x^3 + 2x^2 + x + 1,
so
  Q(x) = Q1(x)Q2(x)
with
  Q1(x)=x^2+1,   Q2(x)=x^2+x+1.

Thus
  P(x) = (x-1)Q1(x)Q2(x).

Step 3 (discriminant_via_iterated_product_and_resultant)
Use the discriminant product rule for monic polynomials:
  Disc(AB) = Disc(A)Disc(B)Res(A,B)^2.

Apply it first to P=(x-1)Q:
  Disc(P) = Disc(x-1) \cdot Disc(Q) \cdot Res(x-1,Q)^2.

Since x-1 is linear and monic, Disc(x-1)=1, so
  Disc(P) = Disc(Q) \cdot Res(x-1,Q)^2.

Step 4 (resultant_evaluation_for_linear_factor)
For a monic linear factor,
  Res(x-r, Q) = Q(r).
Here r=1, so
  Res(x-1,Q) = Q(1).

Compute
  Q(1) = 1 + 1 + 2 + 1 + 1 = 6.
Hence
  Disc(P) = Disc(Q) \cdot 6^2 = 36 \cdot Disc(Q).

Step 5 (compute Disc(Q) using its quadratic factorization)
Again use Disc(AB)=Disc(A)Disc(B)Res(A,B)^2 on Q=Q1Q2:
  Disc(Q) = Disc(Q1)Disc(Q2)Res(Q1,Q2)^2.

Compute quadratic discriminants:
- For Q1(x)=x^2+1, Disc(Q1)=0^2-4 \cdot 1=-4.
- For Q2(x)=x^2+x+1, Disc(Q2)=1^2-4 \cdot 1=-3.

Compute the resultant Res(Q1,Q2). Since Q1 is monic with roots i and -i,
  Res(Q1,Q2) = Q2(i) \cdot Q2(-i).
Now
  Q2(i)=i^2+i+1 = (-1)+i+1 = i,
  Q2(-i)=(-i)^2+(-i)+1 = (-1)-i+1 = -i,
so
  Res(Q1,Q2)= i \cdot (-i)=1.

Therefore
  Disc(Q) = (-4)(-3) \cdot 1^2 = 12.

Step 6 (finish and reduce mod 1000)
Now
  Disc(P) = 36 \cdot Disc(Q) = 36 \cdot 12 = 432.
Thus
  Disc(P) mod 1000 = 432.

Answer: 432.

Uniqueness Test
1) Disc(P) is uniquely determined by P:
The discriminant is a deterministic integer-valued polynomial function of the coefficients of P (equivalently Disc(P)=(-1)^{n(n-1)/2}Res(P,P')/a_n for degree n with leading coefficient a_n). Hence Disc(P) is unique for the given P.

2) Factorization-based computation cannot change the value:
Even though we computed Disc(P) using factorization and product/resultant identities, those identities are equalities in ℤ (via ℚ) and therefore yield the same unique Disc(P). In particular, any valid factorization path must produce the same integer 432.

3) Reduction modulo 1000 is unique:
Every integer has a unique representative in {0,1,...,999} modulo 1000. Since Disc(P)=432, its canonical residue mod 1000 is uniquely 432.

432
